data.title: Workday: Solid Q1 Revenue, Earnings Beat, Stock Gets Punished For Cautious Guidance — These Analysts Revise Price Target
data.description:
Workday Inc WDAY tanked after the company on Thursday reported its fiscal first-quarter results.

The announcement came amid an exciting earnings season. Here are some key analyst takeaways.

KeyBanc Capital Markets On Workday

Analyst Jason Celino reiterated an Overweight rating, while reducing the price target from $335 to $325.

Workday reported 12-month subscription backlog (cRPO) growth of 15.6%, above Street expectations of 15.1%, Celino said in a note. He added, however, that this includes "0.5 points of contribution from ST tenant contracts, which were not previously included in the cRPO definition."

Management's cRPO growth guidance of 15%-16% for the fiscal second quarter also includes this contribution, representing "a slight miss on an apples-to-apples basis," the analyst wrote. He added that the price target is being lowered to reflect "incremental macro uncertainty."

Piper Sandler On Workday

Analyst Brent Bracelin maintained a Neutral rating, while raising the price target from $230 to $255.

Workday delivered a topline beat of $9 million, versus $8 million in the prior four quarters, Bracelin said. Its revenues grew by 13.4% year-on-year, ahead of Piper Sandler's 13.0% estimate, he added.

Subscription revenue came in at $2.059 billion, the analyst stated. "Operating margin came in strong at 30% (vs. 26% last quarter) relative to the 28% guide, partially driven by moderated headcount growth," he further wrote.

Goldman Sachs On Workday

Analyst Kash Rangan reaffirmed a Buy rating, while lifting the price target from $275 to $300.

Workday recorded subscription revenue growth of 13.4% and operating margins of 30.2%, both higher than consensus estimates, Rangan said. The stock came under pressure due to management's lower-than-expected guidance for the fiscal second quarter, of 13.5% subscription revenue growth versus consensus of 13.7%, he added.

The market reaction appears "overblown," given that Workday's revenue growth is back-half weighted, largely driven by already signed deals, while "continued prudence in the face of macro uncertainty makes sense," the analyst wrote.

WestPark Capital On Workday

Analyst Curtis Shauger reiterated a Buy rating, while raising the price target from $315 to $328.

Workday delivered a performance that "demonstrated solid growth and exceptional profitability," Shauger said.

Although the company reported in-line revenues of $2.24 billion, its earnings of $2.23 per share came in significantly ahead of the consensus of $2.01 per share, the analyst stated. The upside was driven by higher-than-expected operating margins, "which we believe reflects the full impact of its recent headcount reductions," he wrote.

Stifel On Workday

Analyst Brad Reback maintained a Hold rating, while cutting the price target from $310 to $275.

Workday's stock plummeted after the results due to cRPO growth coming at the midpoint of management’s guided range and was aided by benefits from short-term tenant contracts "that were not historically included in cRPO," Reback said. "Going forward, these tenant contracts are expected to contribute a full 1pt of uplift to cRPO growth for each of the remaining FY26 quarters," he added.

Investors had expected Workday's core business to record an acceleration in bookings, the analyst stated. "With subscription/bookings growth likely to remain in the mid-to-low-teens area in the coming quarters, even as AI bookings accelerate, we do not expect op-margin upside alone to drive meaningful multiple expansion from current levels," he further wrote.

Check out other analyst stock ratings.

RBC Capital Markets On Workday

Analyst Rishi Jaluria maintained an Outperform rating and price target of $340.

The company reported revenue of $2,240 million, representing 12.6% year-on-year growth, beating consensus of $2,218 million, Jaluria said. "Partner traction continued in Q1, with partners representing more than 20% of net new ACV (up nicely from 15% in Q4)," he wrote.

Workday's non-GAAP earnings came in at $2.23 per share, "nicely above consensus" of $2.01 per share, the analyst stated. He added, however, that the total revenue beat was just by 1.0%, "driven by better-than expected services revenue and a slight beat on subscription revenue."

BofA Securities On Workday

Analyst Brad Sills reaffirmed a Buy rating and price target of $295.

The company, including tenant deals in cRPO, raised questions, Sills said. He added, however, that Workday's transparency is appreciable and that the including is not "a negative, given the recurring nature of these deals."

The company achieved healthy results across geographies and executed well on generating balanced growth, the analyst stated. "The company is on pace to deliver a solid 260 basis points margin expansion this year, driven by the reduction in force and efforts to hire in lower cost geographies," he further wrote.

Citizens On Workday

Analyst Patrick Walravens reiterated a Market Outperform rating and price target of $315.

Workday's first-quarter subscription revenue growth marks a deceleration from 15% in the previous quarter, Walravens said. However, management reiterated their subscription revenue guidance for the second quarter of $2.16 billion, representing 13.5% year-on-year growth, he added.

The company guided to non-GAAP operating margin of around 28.0%, better than consensus of 27.6%, the analyst stated. The full-year subscription revenue of $8.80 billion represents 14% year-on-year growth, he mentioned.

Needham On Workday

Analyst Scott Berg maintained a Buy rating and price target of $300.

The company's cRPO growth reflects new tenants, which was not reflected in the 14.5%-15.5% guidance, Berg said. "AI sales continue to gain momentum and were included in 25% of 1Q expansion deals," he wrote.

Although management's commentary was broadly in-line with expectations, suggesting no material impact of macro uncertainty, the analyst stated. He added, however, that the guidance "reflects continued conservatism on the macro."

Guggenheim Securities On Workday

Analyst John DiFucci reaffirmed a Neutral rating on the stock.

Workday's results reflected "a challenging IT spending environment," DiFucci said. About half the beat on subscription growth was due to a positive currency impact, he added.

This is why the second-quarter guidance came just in-line with expectations and "none of the F1Q beat flowed through to annual guide," the analyst stated.

WDAY Price Action: Shares of Workday had declined by 12.04% to $239.32 at the time of publication on Friday.

data.title: SAP Shares Fall As Trump Tariff Threat Spurs Fears For Transatlantic Tech Trade
data.description:
SAP SE SAP stock fell 1.71% to $294.73 Friday morning, part of a broader market selloff triggered by President Donald Trump's threat to impose a sweeping 50% tariff on all European Union imports starting June 1. The potential trade barrier reignited fears of a renewed transatlantic trade war, sending European equities tumbling.

What To Know: Investors punished export-sensitive stocks, particularly those with major U.S. exposure. SAP, a German enterprise software giant, derives approximately 40% of its revenue from North America.

The company's cloud-based business model and extensive client base in U.S. industries such as finance, healthcare, and manufacturing make it especially vulnerable to U.S.-EU trade disruptions.

The proposed tariffs would exclude U.S.-manufactured goods, putting EU-based firms like SAP at a competitive disadvantage. Analysts warned that retaliatory EU measures could further dent demand or complicate pricing for SAP's cloud services and software solutions.

SAP's drop aligns with losses across Germany's DAX index, down 2.3%, and tech-exposed firms like Infineon and ASML, reflecting the market's swift repricing of risk amid geopolitical uncertainty.

Read Also: Trump Threatens Tim Cook To Manufacture iPhones In US Or Face ‘At Least 25%’ Tariffs

How To Buy SAP Stock

By now you're likely curious about how to participate in the market for SAP – be it to purchase shares, or even attempt to bet against the company.

Buying shares is typically done through a brokerage account. You can find a list of possible trading platforms here. Many will allow you to buy “fractional shares,” which allows you to own portions of stock without buying an entire share.

In the case of SAP, which is trading at $294.73 as of publishing time, $100 would buy you 0.34 shares of stock.

If you're looking to bet against a company, the process is more complex. You'll need access to an options trading platform, or a broker who will allow you to “go short” a share of stock by lending you the shares to sell. The process of shorting a stock can be found at this resource. Otherwise, if your broker allows you to trade options, you can either buy a put option, or sell a call option at a strike price above where shares are currently trading – either way it allows you to profit off of the share price decline.

According to data from Benzinga Pro, SAP has a 52-week high of $303.40 and a 52-week low of $179.43.

data.title: Ex-NBA Star Lamar Odom Booed By Protesters At Trump's Crypto Investor Gala
data.description:
Former NBA star Lamar Odom encountered boos at a dinner for top investors in President Donald Trump’s cryptocurrency venture on Thursday. Protesters outside Trump’s club in Sterling, Virginia, heckled Odom and other attendees.

What To Know: Demonstrators shouted “Hypocrite!” and displayed signs like “Don the Con” and “No Kings” as Odom entered the venue, according to USA Today.

The event was for top purchasers of the $TRUMP meme coin TRUMP/USD and offered exclusive access to the president. Odom, who also promotes his own meme coin, expressed his excitement about attending the gala on social media.

Democrats and ethics experts have raised concerns about Trump’s cryptocurrency activities, suggesting they provide wealthy individuals undue influence over the president. 

A group of congressional Democrats has requested the Justice Department to investigate potential violations of federal bribery laws or the Constitution’s emoluments clause.

Disclosure: 82% of retail CFD accounts lose money

White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt dismissed these allegations, calling them “absurd.” Despite the White House not releasing the guest list, some attendees, including Odom, publicly acknowledged their presence at the event.

Why It Matters: The incident underscores the ongoing controversies surrounding Trump’s cryptocurrency ventures and their ethical implications. Critics argue that such events allow wealthy investors to gain undue influence over the former president, raising questions about transparency and accountability.

The emoluments clause in the Constitution prohibits U.S. officials from accepting gifts from foreign states, and concerns have been raised about whether Trump’s crypto dinner violates this provision.

Furthermore, the presence of high-profile figures like Odom at the event draws attention to the intersection of celebrity, politics and cryptocurrency, highlighting the complex dynamics at play.

As the debate over the ethical implications of Trump’s crypto activities continues, the incident serves as a reminder of the broader challenges facing the regulation and oversight of digital currencies in the political sphere.

  • Bitcoin Breaks $111,000 As Experts Hail ‘Regime Shift’ – Benzinga

Photo: Shutterstock

data.title: UGG And HOKA Parent Deckers Stock Is Falling Friday: What's Going On?
data.description:
UGG and HOKA parent Deckers Outdoor Corporation DECK shares are trading lower Friday. The company reported fourth-quarter earnings after the close Thursday and issued weak guidance for the first quarter, while opting not to issue full-year guidance.

What To Know: The company reported earnings per share of $1.00, beating the consensus estimate of 59 cents. In addition, the company reported sales of $1.02 billion, beating the consensus estimate of $1.01 billion and representing a 7.5% growth year-over-year.

The company broke down sales further, reporting UGG brand sales increased 3.6% year-over-year to $374.3 million. HOKA brand sales increased 10% to $586.1 million and other brand net sales decreased 6.3% to $61.3 million.

Stefano Caroti, President and CEO, said, “While the global trade environment has introduced greater near-term uncertainty, we are very confident in the exciting opportunities ahead for HOKA and UGG. We view these brands as industry leaders, each with iconic and innovative products that operate in differentiated marketplaces. Alongside Deckers’ superb balance sheet, this positions us well to manage through the near-term with a focus on the long-term.”

Cynthia Davis has been named chair of the board, stepping in for Michael Devine, who is retiring after 14 years of service.

Outlook: The company sees first-quarter earnings per share from 62 cents to 67 cents, versus the consensus estimate of 81 cents. Furthermore, it sees sales from $890 million to $910 million, versus the consensus estimate of $925.86 million.

Deckers held back on issuing fiscal-year guidance due to macroeconomic uncertainty.

Analyst Changes: Following the earnings report, multiple analysts issued price target adjustments.

  • Needham analyst Tom Nikic maintained a Buy rating on Deckers and lowered the price target from $150 to $120.
  • Telsey Advisory Group analyst Dana Telsey downgraded Deckers from an Outperform rating to a Market Perform rating and lowered the price target from $240 to $120.

DECK Price Action: At the time of publication, Deckers stock is trading 19.6% lower at $101.41, according to data from Benzinga Pro.

Image: via Shutterstock

data.title: Here's How Much You Would Have Made Owning Casey's General Stores Stock In The Last 15 Years
data.description:
Casey's General Stores CASY has outperformed the market over the past 15 years by 6.34% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 18.2%. Currently, Casey's General Stores has a market capitalization of $16.17 billion.

Buying $100 In CASY: If an investor had bought $100 of CASY stock 15 years ago, it would be worth $1,210.73 today based on a price of $444.05 for CASY at the time of writing.

Casey's General Stores's Performance Over Last 15 Years

Finally -- what's the point of all this? The key insight to take from this article is to note how much of a difference compounded returns can make in your cash growth over a period of time.

This article was generated by Benzinga's automated content engine and reviewed by an editor.
